text:
I'll make the case for one, fully and without hedging, since that's what "make the case" calls for. The sea is lovelier, and the reason is motion. A mountain is a magnificent fact, but it is a finished one. It was lifted into place by forces that stopped working on it a long time ago, and now it simply persists, the same blue silhouette your grandparents saw and your grandchildren will see. Its beauty is the beauty of permanence, which is real but also a little austere, a little indifferent to you. You come and go; the mountain does not notice. The sea, by contrast, is never twice the same. It is the only landscape that is also an event. Every wave is a sentence the water has never spoken before and will never speak again, and the whole surface is busy from horizon to shore composing a poem it immediately erases. To look at the sea is to watch something alive in the act of living, and that is a more intimate kind of loveliness than watching something merely endure.

There is also the matter of the senses. A mountain gives you mostly your eyes, and from a distance at that. The sea gives you everything at once. It gives you the sound, that enormous patient breathing that quiets the mind better than any silence, because pure silence makes us anxious and the sea's sound reassures us that the world is still going about its business. It gives you the smell of salt and weed and weather. It gives you the cold shock and lift of the water on your skin, the taste of it on your lips, the strange lightness of your own body held up by something that asks nothing of you. Mountains you admire; the sea you can enter. Loveliness you can step inside of is a richer thing than loveliness you can only photograph.

And the sea carries a quality mountains can't quite manage: it is both terrifying and tender in the same glance, and it changes its mood while you watch, from glittering flatness to grey fury and back, so that it mirrors the whole emotional range of a human life rather than one frozen note of grandeur. It is the edge of the known world, the horizon where every voyage and every story of leaving begins. Mountains wall you in; they are barriers, the thing the road has to go around. The sea opens out. It is the direction of departure and return, of trade and exile and homecoming, and so it has been wound into our myths and longings far more deeply than any peak. We do not say someone has the call of the mountains in their blood. We say the sea.

So: the sea. Not because mountains lack glory, but because the sea offers a beauty that moves, that you can touch and hear and taste, that shifts with your own moods and invites you in and points you outward toward everywhere you have not yet been. That is the lovelier thing.
